content:
# Revelation Chapter 3 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, pages 279-280 | **Source Image:** REV_003_page_001.jpg, REV_003_page_002.jpg

**1** "To the angel of the church in Sardis, write: These are the words of the one who holds the seven spirits of God and the seven stars: I know your works. You have a reputation for being alive, but you are dead.

**2** Wake up! Strengthen what remains before it dies completely, for I have not found your works complete in the sight of my God.

**3** Remember, then, what you received and heard. Hold on to it and turn back. If you do not wake up, I will come like a thief, and you will have no idea at what hour I will come upon you.

**4** Yet you have a few people in Sardis who have not stained their clothes. They will walk with me dressed in white, for they are worthy.

**5** The one who overcomes will be dressed in white like them. I will never erase that person's name from the book of life, and I will acknowledge that name before my Father and before his angels.

**6** Let anyone who has ears listen to what the Spirit is saying to the churches."

**7** "To the angel of the church in Philadelphia, write: These are the words of the Holy One, the True One, who holds the key of David -- who opens and no one can shut, who shuts and no one can open:

**8** I know your works. Look -- I have placed before you an open door that no one can shut. I know that you have little strength, yet you have kept my word and have not denied my name.

**9** Watch -- I will make those from the synagogue of Satan, who claim to be Jews but are liars, come and fall at your feet, and they will know that I have loved you.

**10** Because you have kept my command to endure, I will also keep you from the hour of trial that is coming upon the whole world, to test everyone who lives on the earth.

**11** I am coming soon. Hold on to what you have, so that no one takes your crown.

**12** The one who overcomes -- I will make that person a pillar in the temple of my God, never again to leave it. I will write on that person the name of my God, and the name of the city of my God -- the new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from my God -- and my own new name.

**13** Let anyone who has ears listen to what the Spirit is saying to the churches."

**14** "To the angel of the church in Laodicea, write: These are the words of the Amen, the faithful and true witness, the origin of all God's creation:

**15** I know your works: you are neither cold nor hot. I wish you were either cold or hot!

**16** So, because you are lukewarm -- neither hot nor cold -- I am about to spit you out of my mouth.

**17** You say, 'I am rich; I have acquired wealth and do not need a thing.' But you do not realize that you are wretched, pitiful, poor, blind, and naked.

**18** I counsel you: buy from me gold refined by fire so you may be truly rich, and white garments so you may be clothed and your shameful nakedness not exposed, and ointment to put on your eyes so you may see.

**19** I discipline and correct everyone I love. So be earnest and turn back.

**20** Look! I stand at the door and knock. If anyone hears my voice and opens the door, I will come in and share a meal with that person, and they with me.

**21** To the one who overcomes, I will give the right to sit with me on my throne, just as I overcame and sat down with my Father on his throne.

**22** Let anyone who has ears listen to what the Spirit is saying to the churches."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

Chapter 3 completes the seven letters with messages to Sardis, Philadelphia, and Laodicea:

- **"You have a reputation for being alive, but you are dead"** (v. 1): The Greek ΟΝΟΜΑ ΕΧΕΙΣ ΟΤΙ ΖΗΣ ΚΑΙ ΝΕΚΡΟΣ ΕΙ -- the contrast between ΟΝΟΜΑ (name/reputation) and reality is devastating. The dynamic rendering foregrounds this gap between public perception and spiritual truth.

- **"Stained their clothes"** (v. 4): ΕΜΟΛΥΝΑΝ ΤΑ ΙΜΑΤΙΑ ΑΥΤΩΝ. Clothing in Revelation carries deep symbolic weight -- purity, identity, worthiness. "Stained" is more visceral and immediate than "soiled" or "defiled."

- **"Fall at your feet"** (v. 9): ΠΡΟΣΚΥΝΗΣΩΣΙΝ ΕΝΩΠΙΟΝ ΤΩΝ ΠΟΔΩΝ ΣΟΥ -- this is a dramatic reversal. The community that has been marginalized will be vindicated. The language echoes Isaiah 60:14.

- **"A pillar in the temple"** (v. 12): ΣΤΥΛΟΝ ΕΝ ΤΩ ΝΑΩ -- in a city prone to earthquakes (Sardis and Philadelphia experienced devastating quakes), the promise of permanence as an immovable pillar in God's temple would carry extraordinary emotional force.

- **"The origin of all God's creation"** (v. 14): Η ΑΡΧΗ ΤΗΣ ΚΤΙΣΕΩΣ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ. The Greek ΑΡΧΗ can mean "beginning," "origin," "ruler," or "source." I chose "origin" to convey Christ's primacy as the source and principle of creation, rather than suggesting he was the first thing created.

- **"Spit you out"** (v. 16): ΕΜΕΣΑΙ ΣΕ -- the Greek verb means "to vomit." "Spit out" is slightly less graphic while retaining the force of the revulsion. Laodicea's lukewarm aqueduct water, arriving tepid from hot springs, makes this metaphor painfully local and concrete.

- **"Share a meal"** (v. 20): ΔΕΙΠΝΗΣΩ ΜΕΤ ΑΥΤΟΥ -- the evening meal (ΔΕΙΠΝΟΝ) was the main meal of the day, the occasion for intimate fellowship. "Share a meal" captures the relational warmth and mutual hospitality better than the formal "dine."

-- PNEUMA
